title: Artificial lighting for winter egg production
subtitle: Extending the laying day to keep hens in production through autumn and winter.
excerpt: Hens slow or stop laying in autumn because the shortening day triggers a hormonal response that pauses egg production. A single low-wattage bulb on a timer, giving 14,16 hours of light per day, overrides that signal and keeps most hens laying through winter. Here's how to set it up safely and what to expect.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Poultry Welfare Codes for laying hens. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. Cornell Small Farms, poultry lighting guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The total daily duration of light experienced by a bird; hens respond to a photoperiod below 14 hours by reducing or stopping egg production.
The annual replacement of feathers, typically in autumn; egg production pauses during moult while the hen's energy goes into feather regrowth.

content.text: Egg production is controlled by light entering the hen's eye, which triggers the hypothalamus to release hormones that stimulate the ovary. When the

drops below around 14 hours, which happens in the UK from late August onwards, the signal weakens and production slows. Adding artificial light at the start or end of the natural day restores the full photoperiod and most hens carry on laying.
Start lighting in August, before the natural day drops below 14 hours, and before the

begins in earnest. Starting the light programme after the moult has triggered means you're trying to restart production from a full pause, which takes longer. Hens that start a light programme in August typically lay through the moult or moult only lightly.
Fit a single low-wattage LED bulb, 9 W is enough for a coop up to 3 m × 3 m. Hens don't need bright light; they need a consistent signal.
Position the bulb centrally in the coop, at head height for a hen on the perch. It needs to reach the back of every hen's eye.

Plug into a timer set to come on before dawn to give a consistent 15,16 hours of total light per day. Morning light works better than evening light, it prevents hens being caught on the ground when the light cuts off suddenly.
Use an outdoor-rated fitting and keep the cable tidy and out of reach. Hens peck cables.

First-year hens on a light programme typically lay through winter with only a short pause, if any. Second-year hens are more variable, some carry on well; others moult lightly and resume quickly. After the third season, production rates drop regardless of lighting.
Don't reduce the light once you've started the programme. A decrease in photoperiod, however small, can trigger a moult mid-winter, which is hard on the hen and disrupts production for weeks.

Hens under continuous lighting with no seasonal rest age their reproductive system faster. A common approach in smallholder flocks is to light for two winters and rest the third, allowing a full moult. Alternatively, let young pullets replace older hens each autumn and keep the older birds unlit for a natural moult and a final laying season.
